speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Peretz Rotenberg. Mr. Rotenberg was born August 17, 1912 in Ciechanow, Poland. I am the interviewer, Lynn Cutler, and we are in Toronto, Ontario in Canada. Today's date is January 31, 1995. The interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my name is Peretz David Rotenberg. In the home, they called me Peretz, and in the street between friends, they called me David Rotenberg. So I got two names.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am born in Ciechanow, 1912, the 17 of August. And-- I am born by parents, where there is merchant, middle-class people. My father was always a merchant, grain merchant they called this, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And lots of Jewish people make their living from this one. They buy it all grains when the farmers came from the farms. They bring grains to the market. And my father went around, you know, and touched the grain-- but then he ordered it bad. It's no good. It have to be dry, you know, because with some farmers, they cut them outside. They went wet, so they had the kilo wet, and when there is dry, it adds three quarter of a kilo. So my father know what kind is good and what not. They send the grain to the mills, and the mills paid my father plus percentage, the difference, how much is the market. If the market, let me say, is about a kilo $2, or 2 zlotys like that, and my father buys them from the farmer for a $1.80-- each one is then my father got plus commission extra. So he makes a living from this one. Since the-- in Poland, started to go around like buyer, but Polaks only. So the farmers, they had got instructions from the church, and I'm saying from the church really, and from the city government, not to say too much to the Jews. So the market started to go around a lot of Christians. They sold them, then another one at the sale, to the Jewish people. So my father felt he lost a lot of [NON-ENGLISH], I like to say, to make a living. He started to buy off himself, grain. And he got that big place like the-- made to hold stock the grain, and they send it to Warsaw. He not send it to the mill, for the mills that are owned, for a time, by the Jews and, after, the Christians buy up the mills. In 1929, the whole world was there in bankruptcy, he'd like to say, just a big depression, they called this. My father was bankrupted too. Before they went bankrupt, my father lost everything to-- what he owned-- even my mother's jewelry. He lost it. My father was very depressed, and my mother, too. But they very much loved to fight. I never saw in my life a father and mother that had arguments of the fight. As a matter of fact, they are married-- they was cousins, when they married. I, myself, seeing what is going on, I ran away from home, half run away, have not. I ran away to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, before you tell me about running away, I just want to know a little bit more about your family, brothers or sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've got one brother and three sisters. There were five children in the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you one of the older or one of the younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no, my older brother, in 1927, he supposed to be registered in the army. My father is a religious man-- not fanatical religious. No, a religious man. He was very afraid my brother would eat no kosher food. And since in the same city in Ciechanow, but the Polish army, and my father knows how they treat the Jews there. The Jewish people, they have Friday. But the Jewish community leader makes the deal with the officers from the camp, from the army camp, to let Friday might, all Jewish soldiers to come to the shul. And every Jew took a soldier home for Shabbat. I'm sorry. [CRYING] To remember everything is really like a dream. My father knew how they treat the soldiers there, and my father didn't want my brother to go to the army, to be treated like that. So my father, was, in that time, [INAUDIBLE] up. This cost him a lot of money, and sent my brother to Canada. Before he sent him to Canada, my father wanted to be sure he will marry some time, the good way, so my brother was engaged and married to a girl from the Molina family. Her maiden name was [NON-ENGLISH], but actually they were [NON-ENGLISH]. After a short time my brother being in Canada, they brang his bride, and were married here officially under the government jurisdiction according to the law. In 1929, when my father went broke, and I went to Lodz, I left my father, my three sisters in the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your sisters older or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they're all younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: All younger.

speaker: interviewee
text: A younger one is named Chaya. Behind Chaya this is Bluma. The very youngest one is Laya. Laya was born in 1929, and Chaya was born in 1916. And Bluma was born in 1920. And, as I said, Laya, the youngster, about 1929. And when they came to Lodz in 1929, they bring a small baby with them. I was there, but bring my parents there, because when I worked, like a tailor, I started to buy two machines, three machines, and I learned my father tailoring, and so my the two sisters started to work, too. We got four machines. They're old machines. They're not electric machines. They are, say, like by foot. And we're working very hard to making pants. And there-- Poland send out a lot of pants for export to the Belgian Congo. So this was a big industry, and the whole industry is in small homes, and poor homes, like to say, where they're working day and night to make a living. And I was there, what to bring them to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were very young when you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very young in that time, very energetic and strong. And believe in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I believe in something I will do for my family, and this holds me very strong and very healthy to be in a family. In general, my mind is a very family mind. I like the family together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're working all together. And, well, I belonged to different organizations, you know, like the young-- and the pogroms in Poland, the [NON-ENGLISH], the big pogrom against Jews, in the false propaganda of the communist. It make me look-- make me dream of something on the other side from the border, where there is a better life for Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after being there, I find out communism and fascism and Nazism, there is more-- there is-- they have more in common, like with the Jewish people. And I learned in a hard way being in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were still young and in large, you were interested in communism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would have to say the truth. I was very interested, because you don't-- pogrom their anti-Semitism. And where you're going, in their sole propaganda there, we feel it like we believe it. And we believe it not because we believe it-- we wanted to believe it's true, because I have got no choice. The discrimination against Jews and the anti-Semitism is so big, and we believe that there is a paradise, and we didn't realize how Stalin and Hitler, they have a common goal against the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, of course, you didn't know that at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time. We are afraid even to look in the other way, not to be disappointed. We've got no choice. We've got nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father interested in communism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, God forbid, no. He's a very religious man, not fanatical religious, but a religious man. He believed in the rabbi-- they regard a strictly kosher home. I'm the only one what revolted because I-- the young generation is like the vision between the older generation and the younger generation. We wanted to believe something, we wanted-- because we've got the-- no choice. We've got nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Palestine, we wanted to believe in Palestine-- now, hard to believe there is-- the Arabs there, and England there, how we can come there? So we started to believe in communism in Russia. Was there a particular organization that you belonged to when you were in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged to the young communist movement. And a matter of fact, I was two-time jailed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were still in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of other Jewish young men with you in the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. There were lots of Jewish people. No, I wanted just to say, it's Polaks, too. I wanted to give you-- let the world know something. In the Polaks, by the Polish people, there is born anti-Semites. The Catholic church-- I wanted this to know the world-- the Catholic church especially pushed the Polaks to be anti-Semite, and I'll give you an example. When I was in Lodz and I was arrested for giving-- for making a demonstration against the government, against fascism, against war, was with me, a Polak. But he is their leader from our factory called Guya's Manufacturers-- Guya's Industrial Fabrics. There's a big, big-- lots of factories there that belong to the Guya family. And he is their leader from the union, and he was a real communist, or he is not a real-- I don't know. No, he was in jail with me together. My father and mother and sister were working very, very hard, because I learned them the tailoring. Every day, they bring me food to the jail. I didn't eat the food. I'd give it to the gentiles that live there, because here's a few Christians there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I give him. He is very skinny. And he eats, maybe, for three-- so I gave him the whole thing which my father and mother didn't eat, and bring it to me. I'd give it to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I tell you the story, when the war came out, everybody said, don't go by train. Go by bus to Russia. I wanted to run away from Lodz. Between this time, my father died. In 1936, my father died. He was sick, a gallbladder infection. And he sent my uncle to a rabbi, but the doctor said, he need an operation. In that time, 1936, the medicine is not so much in the know about medical things like they know now. So my father sent a cousin out to a rabbi to ask the rabbi his idea, his blessing. The rabbi said, you not needed an operation. It will be all right. And three days later, my father died-- with the blessing from the rabbi. In May of 1939, when the war came and I ran away from home-- after when I got-- I don't know if I've told you, but the Germans came in. The Germans wanted some 50 people to clean up the police station. And I'm between the 50 people. We scrubbed the floors and everything, we cleaned. They hold us from 7 o'clock in the morning to 8 o'clock in the night with no food, no drink, no nothing. By 8 o'clock, they took us out in the police backyard there. And one German ate an Apple. And the core of an apple, the middle, they throw it away on the floor. They told me, pick up Jude. When I bent down to pick up the piece of apple, they get me the boot right in the ear. And momently, I lost my vision, my everything. I fell to the floor. Some from the friend, from the Jewish, picked me up, and blood-- some white stuff came out from mine ear. I lost the hearing after now, because it's 8 o'clock and nobody's allowed to go after 8 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next day, I went to see Dr. Rosenberg. I went to him and I show him, and he said, oh, boy. Your ear, you've lost your hearing and nothing they can do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I'm going to the police, tell him why I did it. He said, don't go. If you go, you never will be back there. He'll shut down the place. Do not go. When I realized what is going on, so I decided to go away, to run away to the other side, to the Russian side.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so this was already when the war had started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I just want to go back a little bit to before the war started. You had run away from home when you were about 17 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about 17, 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Did your parents know that you were in Lodz when you went away? In the beginning, no because I hide from them where I am. No, our own merchant came to Lodz. Lodz is the center for textile. And a merchant came from the city to buy some textile, and walking down the street, he recognized me. He said, oh, you're Favel's son. What are you doing here? You know, your parents are looking for you? I said, shh, don't tell them. He said, no. I'm going to tell your parents. When I realized my parents would know, I decided to send home, every week, 10 zlotys. I got, excuse me, I got, by the tailors I work in, I got food, everything-- sleeping quarters, and everything, and washing the linen. And I worked there like their own son. They haven't got children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me in, they get me everything plus 20-- in the beginning $15. Now after $20, 20 zlotys a week, so I sent $10 home. And $10 I needed to smoke, I need for a book, for a paper, for something else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after my father came, you know, we cried. and it started till 1929 when they went in bankruptcy, and everything to Lodz, decided to move to Lodz. And I rent for them a room in Lodz, a big room, where we lived. And when they came, and my father lost even the jewelry, but he gave it to my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we buy a machine, and my father learned very fast-- my father is a very talented person. So he learned the trade. He learned to cut-- to be a cutter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we cut-- the factory, we cut textile. We cut shirts and pants and made all in the home. I've got the two sisters, there the bigger one, the smallest one, the baby not, but she's a baby. Not like the own sister, she's not alive-- what she died, by the-- through the Germans. We working all three by machines. And we so produced pants and shirts for the factory. And so we made a living. We're working about 14, 15 hours a day, actually. No, we made the living how. You know, a poor life, or we make the living. And when the [NON-ENGLISH] came out, my father died in 1936, he died. And so I worked with my sisters, and my mother worked there at the time, too-- just to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents think of you being with the young communists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a very educated man, and my mother too. You can see in the letter in the writing, you know, and my father, too. So they didn't believe it. But they're-- they didn't-- but they're too religious to believe as communism is the thing that will relieve the troubles for the Jewish people. And many young people thinking otherwise. We read a lot. The propaganda, after we find out it's false. You know, it's not true. It's so big, we touch it like it's true, everything. People would run away from Poland to Russia, they send letters, they send how good it is. And I will tell you a story, and I wanted the whole world to know the letter is not here. When I ran away to Russia and being in Russia and realized the truth, as everything that we hear, the propaganda is false and liars. I start to send letters to my brother, but he's in Canada. And I begged him to send me something, and I never got answered. One time I sent a letter. My father died in 1936, remember. One time I decided to send a letter, I was sure the letter would come to Canada. It will go. And I said, these are the words, which the letter is here now. 'Dear, my dear brother and sister-in-law, I'm here in Russia. And I feel so good. We're out of the war. I just feel sorry why I didn't bring my mother and sisters. And soon I will go to Daddy, because I not can more be here, and not see the Daddy.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the letter came, my brother goes, what is he going to come? So they'd get a few friends for, Peter, you [INAUDIBLE], it was so good, he wanted to die. And then they realized, their letter went through-- you see? I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: The letter is still here, it's a document. The communists didn't realize that this is a trick. I make it, you know. So not one letter, they received. I sent them-- every second, third day, I sent a letter, not one letter went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Their letter went through, you see? Because our lovely Stalin, our god. No other god like the god, Stalin. This is communism.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then being in Russia and seeing the truth. I started to cry myself. I said, where are my years, my long, young years, I left for nothing, and believed in something like that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that your parents were pretty religious people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they send you to cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, when I was young, they sent me to cheder. And a little later, they sent me to school. No-- In Ciechanow, I was there, it's not officially a numerus clausus. But every city, every province practiced numerus clausus in somehow, some way. I remember by the-- the third or the fourth day being in school, public school-- all schools, public schools, that are under the jurisdiction from the Catholic church. They are not Catholic. They've got the religious school, Catholic, or there is under the administration of the government. And the government is a Catholic government. Poland must have had a public somehow. Or the religion, the Catholic religion got their pressure, and they won. So by the third day, the teacher, I still seeing her with eye-- they've got the bench there. They're not single like here, in the school-- sit, you know, a chair, let me tell you. There's a-- the whole-- the long one would sit 10 or there are 8 people. And you know, it's like you see in the-- in the shul, so like that one, with a table. So they're not separate sitting. So they pushed me out. They told me to go home. They will call me when [INAUDIBLE]. Some kids from the farm come in. And while there, it's Christians and Catholics, they got the number one. So it's a quarter, like to say, not officially, not legal-- and it's legal because every province did it at its own way. So I was pushed out from the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was either eight or nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were just nine years old, you were pushed out of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or 10, something like that. 10 years old. And life from the beginning, you know, when I was in school. In the beginning, I started the cheder. In the cheder, they just learned us religious. There was another school that belonged to the Mizrahi. Their got-- they learned there, and they learn like the-- how to do something like arithmetic in Polish, you know. When I-- when I-- when they took me out, my parents took me out from the school, my-- I just learned the basic things-- arithmetic, you know, and spelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not that other things what we needed in life, like science and physics. They said, you not need it. What a Jew needed, when he is growing up? Just to have children, to have a family, and to make business, and to know 2 times 2 is 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's 60, 70, 80 years ago. It was a different time and a different world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to the chedar?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I never went to school back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but you went to the cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to cheder, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I started to go to the Yeshiva after cheder. The every-- after the fifth year in the Yeshiva, my soul is just there, or my mind was not there. I'm saying that through-- I started to read. I started to read socialist books. I started to read Karl Marx books. I started to read you know, Karl Marx. And I started to think, not to know, or to think that there is right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you reading these books? Were they in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they're in Jewish and Polish, some Polish and some Jewish, you know. Well, actually, Poland is a democratic country, not a parliament. The communism is not legal, or you've got a communist representative in the parliament, but they cannot arrest him. They cannot touch him. It's a democratic A democratic parliament, it's a democratic country. It was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even though you couldn't go to the public school, you were educating yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, myself, you know, reading a lot. Mostly I read a lot of books. I read a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you getting them from the library?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot from the library, a lot, you know-- I buy, sometimes, books. And we've got in Lodz a very big library, you know, a very big one-- of Jewish and Polish books. And the Jewish literature in Poland is a very big-- Poland was the center from the Jewish literature, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you reading other books besides these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. I read a lot-- I read a lot. Oh, well, that's life. Time has changed. Life has changed. Environment has changed. And your mind and thinking has changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can see that the reading meant a lot to you then and probably continue to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I can say, I read a lot. I read about Marxism a lot. A lot from Marxism.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that in the next take.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believed in this one, and I didn't know I'm fooled.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's all for this tape, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
